Mountain of Fire and Miracles Ministries
By Oluwasegun Aluko, Obafemi Awolowo University
Entry tags: Religious Group, Christian Traditions, Pentecostal, Abrahamic, African Religion, Language, IndoEuropean, Classical Indo-European, Middle-Modern English, Macro-English, Yoruba, New Religiosity, Christian
Mountain of Fire and Miracles Ministries (MFM) is achurch born outof asimple prayer meeting by Dr Daniel
Kolawole Olukoya in 1989, in his own living room in Lagos, Nigeria. What started as asmall, intimate gathering
of believers has blossomed into abig church that is notonly known in Nigeria but also outside the shores of
the country. From those humble beginnings, MFM has grown to over 300 churches within Nigeria and
branches in African and Western countries. The church is afull gospel ministry devoted to the revival of
Apostolic signs, Holy Ghost fire, and deliverance; apraying church committed to spiritual warfare, holiness,
and the power of God to change lives. What makes MFM stand out is its passionate, hands-on approach to
faith – the "do-it-yourself gospel ministry, where people'shands are trained to wage war and their fingers to
do battle." It is apopular charismatic and Pentecostal African-initiated ministry that promotes the prosperity
gospel withemphasis on deliverance ministry and prayers against the works of demonic forces. Armed with
the knowledge that nothing happens without the spiritual being involved, it encourages people, and
especially its members, to actively take charge of their spiritual lives, wrestle through prayer, and fight for
their breakthroughs. Deliverance is of utmost importance in every situation. Beyond prayer and deliverance,
MFM alsonurtures the whole person. The ministry is known for its dedication to music (including orchestras
and guitar choirs) and sports. It also established Mountain Top University, which operates with the conceptof
developing the total man, encompassing the training of body, mind, and spirit. At its various branches,
including the popular “Prayer City” located along the popular Lagos-Ibadan Expressway, Ogun State, Nigeria,
worshippers and those seeking help kept coming, leading to avery large congregation. It is acommunity of
passionate believers who are committed to prayer, holiness, and the belief that no situation is too difficult for
God to transform. Whether through fiery midnight prayers, heartfelt worship, or practical community
engagement, MFM continues to be aspiritual home for millions seeking hope, healing, and adeeper walk
with God.
Date Range: 1989 CE - 2025 CE
Region: Nigeria
Region tags: Africa, Nigeria
Nigeria, West Africa. Most Nigerian Pentecostals live in
southern Nigeria.

Entry Perspective
✓ Orthodoxy Sources

Mountain of Fire and Miracles Ministries", Journal of World Christianity, Vol. 5, No. 1, pp. 75–101. — Source 2:: Oluwasegun Peter Aluko and Oluwatoyin Dorcas Peter, "Evil Conception and

African Journal of Religion, Philosophy and Culture (AJRPC), Vol. 4, No. 2, pp 65-86.
— Source 3:: Adewale Adelakun, Understanding Sexuality from the Security Gospel Perspective:

73, No. 3, p. a3453. https://doi. org/10.4102/hts.v73i3.3453 Media and third sector sources related to the subject matter: ID: 11404 Attach PDFs if available in digital format.

Attach PDFs if available in digital format.
— Source 1:: The Holy Bible
— Source 2:: Prayer Rain, frequently referred to by scholars as the "MFM Manual"
— Source 3:: The Seventy Days Prayer and Fasting Booklet
Notes: The church relies on these sources, including the Bible. However, the interpretation is ably one that
is given by the founder. The "prayer points" in these books often take on aritualistic power that mimics the
sacraments but lacks the historical continuity of the Apostolic Church. List the locations of any key archives relating to this movement ID:
11406 — List locations:: MFM International Headquarters (Onike, Yaba); Prayer City (Lagos-Ibadan
Expressway); Mountain Top University (MTU) Library; The British Library & UK Charity Commission (London,
UK) Aluko, Database of Religious History, Page 2 of 2026 121 — List locations:: University of Birmingham (Cadbury Research Library, UK)
Notes: Known for its "Special Collections" on African-led churches and Pentecostalism, this library is akey
site for researchers studying the intersection of West African spirituality and global Charismatic
movements. General Variables
Date Range
On what basis was the date range selected? ID: 9727 — Founding period
— Theological development
Notes: The MFM was founded in 1989 as a "spiritual ambulance" for a Nigerian society in socio-economic
crisis, centring its identity on aggressive prayer and the total deliverance of the believer from ancestral
bondages. From an Orthodox perspective, this founding period marks asignificant shift from the
liturgical stability of the historic Church toward a "prophetic instrumentalism", where the God and the
Holy Spirit are invoked primarily as adestructive fire against spiritual enemies. Consequently, the
movement’sorigin reflects amodern, charismatic response to the unseen world, prioritising immediate
breakthrough over the traditional, ascetic path of gradual deification.

Religious Tradition
Is this entry focused on atransnational religious movement? ID: 9728 Transnational - addressing an audience that transcends nation state identity or aparticular ethnic/regional
diasporic population — Yes
Notes: The church has, since 1989 and subsequent years, become aquintessential transnational
movement. It has expanded from its 1989 Lagos origins to establish a "prayer fire" across over 70 countries
viathe African diaspora. While maintaining acentralised "Apostolic" authority in Nigeria, its global reach
relies on digital media and satellite broadcasting to export astandardised liturgy of deliverance to
diverse cultural contexts. From an Orthodox perspective, this transnationalism represents a "network
ecclesiology" that contrasts with the ancient, territorial, and autocephalous structure of the Orthodox
Church, which is rooted in localised episcopal succession rather than aglobal charismatic franchise. Is this entry focused on aglobal religious movement? ID: 9729 Aluko, Database of Religious History, Page 3 of 2026 121 Global - addressing aparticular ethnic or national diasporic identity that is dispersed globally — Yes
Notes: The entry is focused on aglobal religious movement, as the church has transitioned from a
localised prayer group in Lagos to apervasive international presence with branches in over 70 countries.
This global expansion is facilitated by a "transnational liturgy" of aggressive prayer and deliverance that
resonates with the African diaspora and increasingly attracts non-African adherents seeking spiritual
solutions to life'schallenges. Is this entry focused on aspecific national contextof the religious movement? ID: 9730

— Yes
Notes: While the movement is globally present, this entry is fundamentally rooted in the Nigerian
national context, as MFM’stheology and liturgy are inextricable from the spiritual and socio-economic
landscape of late 20th-century West Africa. Its core tenets, like "deliverance" from ancestral yokes, directly
address the Yoruba worldview of the "unseen world" and the specific pressures of the Nigerian postcolonial era. Regardless of scholarly classification, does the religious movement understand itself
as partof alarger religious tradition? ID: 9731 — Yes
Notes: The MFM understands itself as avital componentof the Global Pentecostal and Holiness tradition,
specifically positioned as a "revivalist" force intended to restore the spiritual militancy of the early Church.
It views its practices not as new inventions but as areturn to the Apostolic mandate of deliverance and
holiness that it believes traditional denominations have abandoned. From an Orthodox perspective, this
is seen as "Restorationism", an attempt to leapfrog two thousand years of Holy Tradition to recreate a
perceived "primitive" Christianity based on personal charisma rather than sacramental continuity. Select all that apply: ID: 9732 — Christian
Notes: Yes, the church understands itself as apartof the larger Christian body. It sees itself as
being an African Pentecostal Christian church that is interested in rooting out evil from the
members' lineages. To the church, evil must not exist in whatever form. Is the religious movement amember of any overarching religious organisation ID:
9733
or tradition?
— Yes Comment: MFM is akey member of the Pentecostal Fellowship of Nigeria (PFN) and Aluko, Database of Religious History, Page 4 of 2026 121

identifies with the global Holiness-Pentecostal tradition, which emphasises the immediate
power of the Holy Spirit and strict moral conduct. It functions as an autonomous "apostolic"
ministry, yet it remains rooted in the Aladura-Pentecostal heritage of West Africa, prioritising
spiritual warfare as the primary expression of faith. From an Orthodox perspective, this
membership represents a "voluntary association" based on shared experience and fervour, rather
than the Organic Unity found in the Orthodox Church through shared creeds, ancient canons,
and the Eucharist. Does the religious movement engage in interfaith work? ID: 9734 — No
Notes: MFM rarely engages in formal "interfaith dialogue" aimed at finding theological commonality, as
its foundational doctrine views non-Christian belief systems as spiritual territories requiring "deliverance"
and conversion. While the ministry participates in civic-level interreligious councils in Nigeriato ensure
social peace, its primary interaction with other faiths remains confrontational and evangelistic. State and Society
Is there violent conflict (within the geographical area under consideration)? ID: 9737 — Yes
Notes: The geographical heartof MFM, Nigeria, is currently experiencing asurge in violent conflict
characterised by jihadist attacks, mass kidnappings, and intercommunal clashes that have left thousands
dead in early 2026. MFM responds to this insecurity by framing it as a "spiritual emergency", urging its
members to engage in "violent faith" and "aggressive intercession" toneutralise the dark forces behind
the physical violence. Does the country have an official state religion or religions? ID:
9738 — No
